content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 17,
1993
Robert J.
Logan
City Attorney
City of Grover City
152 North Third Street,
Suite 201
San Jose,
CA
95112
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-l00
Dear Mr.
Logan:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of members of the city council,
planning commission and
city staff of Grover City regarding their responsibilities under
the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May city officials who
own property in and around the Grover
Beach Redevelopment Area participate in decisions regarding
formulation of the redevelopment plan?
CONCLUSION
Neither Ms. Risoldi nor Mr.
Johnson have an economic interest
involved in the decisions and therefore they may participate in
the decisions.
Councilmembers Forister and Gates,
and Planning Commissioners
Reed,
Coe,
Geiss,
Hall and Zionek may participate pursuant to
Regulation 18703.1,
so long as their repective residences are no
more than one—quarter acre in size or not larger than
125 percent
of the median residential 1t size for the jurisdiction.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-lOO
Page
2
Ms.
Cuibreth,
Mr. Picquet,
Mr.
Sullivan,
Councilmember Munroe
and Commissioner Roberts may participate in the decision so long
as the effect of the decision on their interests will be
indistinguishable from the effect of the decisions on a
significant segment of Grover City.
If the decisions will have a material financial effect on the
interests of Councilmember Arnoidsen and Councilmember Keith,
they
may not participate in the decisions.
FACTS
Grover City is a small city of 11,901 persons and covering
less than ten square miles.
The city is in the earliest stages of
formulating a redevelopment plan for the Grover Beach
Redevelopment Area
(the “project area”).
The project area encompasses
44 percent of the acreage of the
city.
You also stated that 41 percent of the residential property
of the city is within the project area,
and 95 percent of the
city’s residential property is within 2,500 feet of the project
area boundaries.
Due to the significant size of the project area,
most of the
elected and appointed officials,
and some of the city staff
members have interests that will be affected by the redevelopment
project.
You have specifically described the following:
Elected and Appointed Officials
Property Interest
Councilmemnber Ron Arnoldsen
Residence is in the project area
and the councilmember also owns a
dental office in project area.
Councilmember Lowell Forister
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Councilmember Gene Gates
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Councilmember Peter Keith
Residence is within 2,500 feet and
owns a variety of property
interests within the project area,
including 16 residential rental
units and one vacant lot.
He is
also purchasing three more rental
units in the project area.
Councilmember Fred Munroe
Residence is within 300 feet.
The
residence is
a unit in a
cooperatively owned building.
Commissioner Robert Reed
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner William Coe
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner John Geiss
Residence is within 2,500 feet.


Our File No.
A-93-100
Page
3
Elected and Appointed Officials
Property Interest
Commissioner Guyla Hall
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner John Zionek
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner Richard Roberts
Residence is within 300 feet.
Agency Staff
Penny Cuibreth
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Tom Sullivan
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Brian Johnson
Rents property within 2,500
feet of the area,
on a monthly
basis.
Roger Picquet
Owns a rental within 2,500 feet.
Patricia Risoldi
Has no interests near the area.
You also stated that the city councilmembers, planning
commissioners and the city manager are required to reside in the
jurisdiction and that the city councilinembers are elected in at—
large elections.
City staff is not required to reside within the
jurisdiction.
ANALYSIS
Conflicts of Interest
The Act provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.
Section 87100.
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:


Our File No.
A—93-100
Page
4
***
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official,
the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(b).
With the exception of two of the officials on whose behalf
you have requested this advice, all of the officials in question
have real property interests pursuant to Section 87103(b) that may
be affected by the redevelopment decisions.
We assume that each
has an interest in their respective properties worth $1,000 or
more.
You stated that Ms. Risoldi does not
own any real property in
proximity to the project area.
Additionally,
you stated that
Brian Johnson rents property,
on a month—to—month basis near the
project area.
While Section 82033 defines “interest in real
property” to include any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest in real property, Regulation 18233 provides that the
terms “interest in real property” and “leasehold interest” as used
in Section 82033
do not include the interest of a tenant in a
periodic tenancy of one month or less.
Thus, neither Ms. Risoldi,
nor Mr.
Johnson have an economic
interest involved in the decision that will result in a conflict
of interest.
However,
the remaining officials in question are
prohibited from making, participating in,
or influencing a deci
sion which will have a foreseeable
2 and material financial effect
on their respective real property interests.
2
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time
a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required
however, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
A-93-100
Page
5
The standard for materiality differs depending on whether
real property is directly or indirectly involved in a decision.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (D)
applies specifically to redevelopment
decisions directly affecting the official’s real property.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (D)
provides that a governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on an official’s
real property interest where:
(D)
The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document,
to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions;
and real property in which the
official has an interest,
or any part of it is
located within the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the redevelopment area.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (D)
Councilinember Arnoidsen owns a residence and his dental
office in the project area.
Additionally,
Councilmember Keith
owns a variety of property interests within the project area,
including
16 residential rental units and one vacant lot.
He is
also purchasing three more rental units in the project area.
Thus,
if the decision before the city council or
redevelopment agency is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan,
to form a project
area committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt
the redevelopment plan,
to add territory to the redevelopment
area,
or to rescind or amend any of the above decisions,
Councilinember Arnoldsen and Councilmember Keith must disqualify
themselves from the decisions.
With respect to all the other officials, materiality is
determined by applying an indirect materiality test.
Regulation
18702.3 provides:
3
(a)
The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest
(not
Regulation 18702.3(b)
provides a standard for materiality that
applies where the real property in which an official has an
interest is located entirely beyond a 2,500 foot radius of the
boundaries of the subject property and there are specific
circumstances regarding the decision which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official has an interest will be
affected by the amounts set forth in subdivisions
(a) (3) (A)
or
(a) (3) (B).
You have not described any real property interest that
would fall within the distance requirements of this provision.


Our File No.
A-93-100
Page
6
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
(1)
The real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that
real property,
is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official’s real property interest.
(2)
The decision involves construction
of,
or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities,
and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.
(3)
The real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a
radius of
300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest;
or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per
12
month period.
***
(e)
Redevelopment Decisions:
For purposes of
this section “the boundaries
(or proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the subject of
the decision” are the boundaries
(or proposed
boundaries)
of the redevelopment project area
whenever the decision is a redevelopment decision
to designate the survey area, to make findings of
blight, to select the project area,
to adopt the
preliminary plan,
to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions.
The residences of Councilmember Munroe and Commissioner
Roberts are within 300 feet of the project boundaries.
Pursuant


Our File No.
A-93-100
Page
7
to Regulation 18702.3(a) (1), the councilmembers may not
participate in decisions to designate the survey area, to make
findings of blight, to select the project area, to adopt the
preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions unless there will be no financial effect upon
the councilmembers’ real property interests.
Councilmembers Forister and Gates,
and Commissioners Reed
Coe, Geiss, Hall and Zionek,
and Ms.
Cuibreth,
Mr.
Sullivan and
Mr. Picquet own residences beyond 300 feet,
but closer than 2,500
feet of the project boundaries.
Thus,
they may not participate in
the decisions described above if the decisions will financially
affect the fair market value of their real property by $10,000 or
more; or will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or
more per
12 month period.
We cannot determine the magnitude of the financial effect on
the various real property interests discussed above.
However,
Regulation 18702.3(d)
sets forth factors that must be considered
in determining whether a decision will have a material financial
effect real property.
4
1.
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship
to the property in which the official has an
interest;
2.
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
3.
[W]hether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will result in a change to the
character of the neighborhood including,
but not
limited to,
the effect on traffic,
view, privacy,
intensity of use,
noise levels, air emissions,
or
similar traits of the neighborhood.
“Public Generally” Exceptions
1.
Regulation 18703.1
Public officials with real property interests that will be
financially affected by a governmental decision may still
participate in the decision if the effect of the decision on their
property is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
For the “public generally” exception to apply,
a
decision must affect an official’s interests in substantially the
The factors to be considered are not limited to the factors
specified in Regulation 18702.3(d).


Our File No.
A-93-100
Page
8
same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public
in the official’s jurisdiction.
(Regulation 18703.)
Regulation 18703.1
(copy enclosed)
provides a special
exception applicable to small jurisdictions.
For the regulation
to apply to your facts, the decision must meet all of the
following:
1.
The decision will affect the principal residence
5 of the
official,
and not any other economic interest.
2.
The jurisdiction must have a population of 25,000 or
less,
and cover a geographic area of ten square miles or less.
3.
The public officials must be required to reside within
the jurisdiction and with respect to elected officials,
must have
been elected in an at—large election.
4.
The decision may not have a direct effect
(as provided in
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3))
on the public official’s principal
residence,
nor may the principal residence of the official be 300
feet or less from the boundaries of the property which is the
subject of the decision.
5.
The principal residence must be located on a parcel of
land not more than one—quarter acre in size or, alternatively,
a
residential lot not larger than 125 percent of the median
residential lot size for the jurisdiction.
You stated that Grover City’s population is less than 25,000
and the city covers a geographic area of less than ten square
miles.
You also stated that the city councilmembers and planning
commissioners must reside in the jurisdiction,
6 and that the city
councilmembers are elected in at—large elections.
Of the city
councilmembers and planning commissions that have property
interests in or near the project area,
seven own personal
Regulation 18703.1(b)
provides:
“For purposes of this
regulation,
“principal residence” means the domicile of a person,
in which the person’s habitation is fixed,
wherein the person has
the intention of remaining,
and to which the person,
whenever he
or she is absent, has the intention of returning.
At any given
time,
a person may have only one principal residence.”
6
City staff is not required to reside within the jurisdiction,
and therefore do not fall within the exception in the regulation.
However,
you stated that the city manager is required to reside in
the jurisdiction.
If the city manager meets the other
requirements of Regulation 18703.1, the city manager may
participate in the decisions.


Our File No. A-93-l00
Page
9
residences that are beyond 300 feet of the boundaries and have no
other economic interests that will be affected by the decision.
7
Thus,
Councilmembers Forister and Gates,
and Planning
Commissioners Reed,
Coe, Geiss, Hall and Zionek appear to meet the
requirements of the exception.
You have not specified the size of
the officials’ respective properties.
However,
so long as
residences of the respective officials is no more than one—quarter
acre in size
(or not larger than 125 percent of the median
residential lot size for the jurisdiction), Regulation 18703.1
will permit the officials to participate.
2.
Regulation 18703
Regulation 18703.1(c)
provides that even if the exception
does not apply, public officials with financial interests that
will be materially affected by a decision may still participate in
the decision if the effect on their property is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally pursuant
to Regulation 18703.
For the “public generally” exception of Regulation 18703 to
apply,
a decision must affect the official’s interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
The “public” consists
of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in question.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
According to your facts,
44 percent of the acreage of the
city is within the redevelopment area.
You also stated that 41
percent of the residential property of the city is within the
project area,
and 95 percent of the city’s residential property is
within 2,500 feet of the project area boundaries.
Ms.
Culbreth
and Mr. Sullivan own residences that are within 2,500 feet of the
project boundaries.
Councilmember Munroe and Commissioner Roberts
own residences which are within 300 feet of the project
boundaries.
In the Eiser Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-559,
we considered the
application of the “public generally” exception to decisions with
respect to National City’s redevelopment plan, which covered 44
percent of the city’s land use area.
We advised that a
councilmeinber who owned a personal residence within the
redevelopment area could participate in the decision because there
were large numbers of private residential homes in the
redevelopment area which would be similarly affected.
Councilmember Arnoidsen owns a dental office in the project
area;
Councilmember Keith owns multiple real property interests in
and around the project area;
Councilmeinber Munroe and Commissioner
Roberts own residences within 300 feet of the project boundaries.
Therefore,
none of these officials meet the requirements of
Regulation 18703.1.


Our File No.
A—93-l00
Page
10
Additionally, in the Haiqht Advice Letter,
No. A-81-509,
we
advised that where a project site encompassed 26 percent of the
city, that persons that owned personal residences in and around
the project area could participate by virtue of the “public
generally” exception.
Finally,
in the Phillips Advice Letter,
No. A-87-166,
we
advised that where a councilinember owned real property within
three homes of the project boundary, the councilmember could
participate in the redevelopment decisions because “there is
substantial residential property located directly outside the
boundary of the proposed project.”
Based on this fact,
we
concluded that “the creation of the proposed project area will not
affect [the councilmember’s) real property in a manner
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.”
Based on this past authority,
and the fact that 41 percent of
the residential property of the city is within the project area,
and 95 percent of the city’s residential property is within 2,500
feet of the project area boundaries,
we conclude that
Ms.
Culbreth,
Mr.
Sullivan,
Councilmeiuber Munroe and Commissioner
Roberts will not be affected in a manner distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally in Grover City,
and may, therefore,
participate in the redevelopment decisions.
8
Mr.
Picquet owns a condominium as rental property within
2,500 feet of the project area.
His personal residence is outside
of the jurisdiction.
While the effect of the redevelopment
decisions may be different on rental property in the redevelopment
area,
where the property is more than 300 feet and less than 2,500
feet from the boundaries of the project area, the effect would
appear to be less unusual.
Moreover, if the rental property was owned in addition to
other real property, the difference of the effect of the decision
on Mr.
Picquet would be greater than the effect on the public
generally.
However,
according to your facts,
Mr.
Picquet only
owns the single parcel in the jurisdiction.
We have no facts to
indicate that the fair market value of Mr. Picquet’s parcel will
not be affected in a similar manner to those residences within
approximately the same distance.
Thus,
we conclude the “public
generally” exception would apply to Mr. Picquet as well.
9
8
Please note that if facts indicate that the interests of the
officials discussed above will be affected in an unusual manner,
due to the unusual size of the property, the “public generally”
exception would not apply.
As we cautioned above, this letter is based on the facts
presented to us.
The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
If facts exist indicating that the Mr. Picquet’s property interest
will be affected in an unusual manner, the “public generally”
exception would not apply.


Our File No.
A—93—l00
Page 11
Councilmeinber Arnoidsen owns a residence and his dental
office in the project area.
Councilme!nber Keith owns a residence
within 2,500 feet of the project area,
and multiple rental units
within the area.
With respect to both these officials,
a material
financial effect on y of their interests will result in
disqualification, unless an exception exists for each of their
interests.
The fact that they own multiple interests that will be
affected by the redevelopment decisions would mean that the effect
of the decisions would be substantially greater on them as opposed
to other officials that own only single family residences.
Thus,
the “public generally” exception would not apply to Councilmeinber
Arnoldsen
10 or Councilmember Keith.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
32259O1.ll
Sincerely
Jeff Marschner
ral Counsel
10
As we stated in our letter to CouncilmeTnber Arnoidsen
(Advice
Letter A—92-66l), the Act also contains a “public generally”
exception for income from retail sales of goods or services.
However, the rendition of dental services would not fall within
this exception.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Conuuission at a
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
cost of 10 per page.

May city officials who
own property in and around the Grover
Beach Redevelopment Area participate in decisions regarding
formulation of the redevelopment plan?

they
may not participate in the decisions.

The
residence is
a unit in a
cooperatively owned building.
Commissioner Robert Reed
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner William Coe
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner John Geiss
Residence is within 2,500 feet.


Our Page
3
Elected and Appointed Officials
Property Interest
Commissioner Guyla Hall
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner John Zionek
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Commissioner Richard Roberts
Residence is within 300 feet.
Agency Staff
Penny Cuibreth
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Tom Sullivan
Residence is within 2,500 feet.
Brian Johnson
Rents property within 2,500
feet of the area,
on a monthly
basis.
Roger Picquet
Owns a rental within 2,500 feet.
Patricia Risoldi
Has no interests near the area.
You also stated that the city councilmembers, planning
commissioners and the city manager are required to reside in the
jurisdiction and that the city councilinembers are elected in at—
large elections.
City staff is not required to reside within the
jurisdiction.

Conflicts of Interest
The Act provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May city officials who
own property in and around the Grover
Beach Redevelopment Area participate in decisions regarding
formulation of the redevelopment plan?

CONCLUSION: Neither Ms. Risoldi nor Mr.
Johnson have an economic interest
involved in the decisions and therefore they may participate in
the decisions.
Councilmembers Forister and Gates,
and Planning Commissioners
Reed,
Coe,
Geiss,
Hall and Zionek may participate pursuant to
Regulation 18703.1,
so long as their repective residences are no
more than one—quarter acre in size or not larger than
125 percent
of the median residential 1t size for the jurisdiction.
et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-lOO
Ms.
Cuibreth,
Mr. Picquet,
Mr.
Sullivan,
Councilmember Munroe
and Commissioner Roberts may participate in the decision so long
as the effect of the decision on their interests will be
indistinguishable from the effect of the decisions on a
significant segment of Grover City.
If the decisions will have a material financial effect on the
interests of Councilmember Arnoidsen and Councilmember Keith,
they
may not participate in the decisions.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission March 17, 1993 Robert J. Logan City Attorney City of Grover City 152 North Third Street, Suite 201 San Jose, CA 95112 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-93-l00 Dear Mr. Logan: This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of members of the city council, planning commission and city staff of Grover City regarding their responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 QUESTION May city officials who own property in and around the Grover Beach Redevelopment Area participate in decisions regarding formulation of the redevelopment plan? CONCLUSION Neither Ms. Risoldi nor Mr. Johnson have an economic interest involved in the decisions and therefore they may participate in the decisions. Councilmembers Forister and Gates, and Planning Commissioners Reed, Coe, Geiss, Hall and Zionek may participate pursuant to Regulation 18703.1, so long as their repective residences are no more than one—quarter acre in size or not larger than 125 percent of the median residential 1t size for the jurisdiction. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-93-lOO Page 2 Ms. Cuibreth, Mr. Picquet, Mr. Sullivan, Councilmember Munroe and Commissioner Roberts may participate in the decision so long as the effect of the decision on their interests will be indistinguishable from the effect of the decisions on a significant segment of Grover City. If the decisions will have a material financial effect on the interests of Councilmember Arnoidsen and Councilmember Keith, they may not participate in the decisions. FACTS Grover City is a small city of 11,901 persons and covering less than ten square miles. The city is in the earliest stages of formulating a redevelopment plan for the Grover Beach Redevelopment Area (the “project area”). The project area encompasses 44 percent of the acreage of the city. You also stated that 41 percent of the residential property of the city is within the project area, and 95 percent of the city’s residential property is within 2,500 feet of the project area boundaries. Due to the significant size of the project area, most of the elected and appointed officials, and some of the city staff members have interests that will be affected by the redevelopment project. You have specifically described the following: Elected and Appointed Officials Property Interest Councilmemnber Ron Arnoldsen Residence is in the project area and the councilmember also owns a dental office in project area. Councilmember Lowell Forister Residence is within 2,500 feet. Councilmember Gene Gates Residence is within 2,500 feet. Councilmember Peter Keith Residence is within 2,500 feet and owns a variety of property interests within the project area, including 16 residential rental units and
